summary:
The current standard recommends initial ophthalmologic screening at or near diagnosis for individuals with Type 2 diabetes, regardless of duration since onset, given the prevalence of undetected retinal damage even at first presentation. This applies immediately—even within the first year—as part of routine care despite historical recommendations suggesting later initiation. 

Note: Do not include any commentary about the answer being correct/incorrect; only summarize what was reasoned out by Agent2.

The recommended guideline is to initiate ophthalmological screening at or around diagnosis for people with Type 2 diabetes because many exhibit detectable retinal abnormalities from day one. Screening should occur promptly upon diagnosis—not delayed—to enable timely intervention against diabetic eye disease progression.  

(Note: Only two sentences are used.)

Okay, let me go through this step-by-step:

First, identify which specific point relates directly to whether someone diagnosed just one year ago needs an exam — it’s clearly tied to when screenings start post-diagnosis.

Next, extract core facts from Agent2’s argument:
- There exists conflict between old vs new standards
- Modern consensus favors immediate screening starting at diagnosis 
- Especially strong support comes from data showing up to ~10% of T2D cases have visible retinopathy right at baseline  
- Even though some outdated literature suggests wait till age 5+ years, newer protocols emphasize earlier testing due to risk level present initially

Now focus strictly on clinically significant takeaways without adding external interpretation:
→ Early screening begins at diagnosis → Applies now especially crucial during first-year period where pathology may be silent but still existent

Final summary must contain exactly TWO concise factual statements reflecting these critical elements while avoiding opinionated language like “should” unless grounded in cited rationale.

Thus final version becomes clean, direct, accurate representation based solely on internal logic presented.
